summary: McKenzie reports that a stressful personal situation has resolved, though not in his favor. The two weeks lost to worrying have put his 30-day development sprint behind schedule, making it unlikely he'll have a saleable application by the deadline. He's resetting his goal to have something publicly demonstrable by the 1st, with an actual release to follow later. He explains why he can't just ship a half-finished product: since widgets live on third-party sites, bugs could break other people's websites.
key_points: Personal stress and worry can consume productive development time just as effectively as technical blockers
When building products that run on third-party sites, the quality bar for shipping is higher because your bugs affect other people's properties
Adjusting launch timelines and setting realistic revised goals is better than pretending the original deadline is still achievable
money_quotes: Sadly, it did not resolve itself to my benefit, but it is no longer hanging in the air, which means I can get on with life.

I would happily launch with a half-finished app that doesn't even have billing integrated, but the nature of widgets is that they exist on third party sites, and half-finished could very well mean that widgets break on third party sites, which is just not good behavior.
key_insight: When your product runs on other people's websites, you can't ship fast and break things — their reputation is on the line, not just yours.
